VI

The Red Lights

I

The shape of her coif against the dim light was like some legendary thingвАЩs head, and she was eating. I heard her. That she was old and very stout was all I could see. I could smell just a little, too. Poor Iris.

I asked if I might have news of Mrs.¬†Storm.

вАЬAh, la dame anglaise!вАЭ She ate, but not finally. вАЬMadame est assez bien, je crois. Mais pardon, monsieur. Je nвАЩai pas dвАЩinstructions √† vous donnerвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬBut!вАЭ I pleaded. вАЬButвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬJe regrette, monsieur. CвАЩest pas ma faute, vous savez. Pardon.вАЭ

She was closing the door! Terse as you like. I was helpless. вАЬMadame est assez bien, je crois!вАЭ Dear Heaven, but didnвАЩt one know those assez biens! IsnвАЩt there a company in Heaven wholly recruited from those who have been assez bien, and daily augmented by those who are assez bien!

I lifted up my voice.

вАЬPardon, monsieur.вАЭ

I lifted up my voice in vain. So I was active. She stared at me, panting. I withdrew my first impression as to her being a nun. She was no nun. She had a crucifix and a coif, but she was no nun. She was a woman scorned. She said many things and used many words which I did not understand. But I didnвАЩt care. I somehow thought, you know, of Iris dying.

вАЬI am here,вАЭ I said in effect, вАЬand here I stay until I can speak to a doctor or a matron. I am sorry, but you have made me anxious as to the ladyвАЩs health.вАЭ

вАЬMais je vous lвАЩai d√©j√† dit, jeune homme! Madame est assez bien!вАЭ

The ordinary dingy conciergeвАЩs lodge: a black stove, a table covered with frayed red cloth, a chair, a stool, an indescribable odour, a plate of food on the tableвБ†вАФb≈Уuf bouilli, which is French for the salvaging of grey matter from liquid dungeons of onions, carrots and potatoes. I sat on the stool. It was unbelievable that her coif had ever been white. Somehow my eyes were transfixed by the small wooden crucifix which, like a dinghy on a choppy sea, rolled on her bosom as she ate. I wondered how long I would have to wait. I wondered if I could smoke. I wondered if this was one of those convent-nursing-homes. I wondered if one called a nun madame or mademoiselle. They were maidens presumably, so I supposed mademoiselle.

вАЬOn peut fumer, mademoiselle?вАЭ

I was wrong. She looked at me with contempt. вАЬCвАЩest d√©fendu, monsieur.вАЭ

вАЬMerci, madame.вАЭ

I wondered if she really could be a nun. I wondered if one could tip a nun. Out of sheer hatred one acquires a passion for tipping in France and Italy. Detestable it was on this detestable day to sit like this, being hated. I made a muttering noise and gave her a ten-franc note, and it was in a more amiable spirit that she went on with her salvaging. At last there were only two bits of carrot and an awful looking onion left to engage her attention, and I felt that one might perhaps converse.

I was right about her being no nun. She was a lay-sister, she said. And this place, she told me, was a convent-nursing-home. вАЬNous avons ici,вАЭ she was pleased to add, вАЬla client√®le europ√©enne la plus chic.вАЭ

Perhaps that was the worst stroke of that day, so far. Iris among a client√®le europ√©enne la plus chic.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ One saw the cosmopolitan divorc√©es, their secret illnesses and guileful pains, their nasty little coquetries and the way they would blackmail their lovers with their sufferings, and one felt the sticky nightclub breath of all the silly, common harlotries of England, France, America. My poor ten-franc note must have seemed pathetic to this old lay-sister, who probably thought nothing of receiving a mille from an anxious Dago.

I had until then been trying not to wonder about Iris in the vile shadow of a prison. Suddenly I was furiously hot. What on earth was I doing here! Intruding where I was not wanted! I was about to go, to run, when the lay-sister was as though distracted from the last piece of carrot by the opening of a door in the back room. Frantically she hurried towards it. It would look too silly of me to run now. I could but ask, anyhow.

The lay-sisterвАЩs voice, voluble, vindictive, explanatory. Much good my ten francs had done! Then steps came towards me, into the lodge. вАЬEh,вАЭ I said. How afraid one always is of the callous French doctors with their cynical eyes and purple beards.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

A man, bald, sharp-featured as a bird, in a rough brown greatcoat, a tired-looking, an anxious-looking, middle-agedвБ†вАФEnglishman!

вАЬMasters! Conrad Masters!вАЭ

вАЬWell,вАЭ muttered that anxious-looking man. He looked just the same when he was playing bridge. He was always playing bridge, that man. And he said he hated playing bridge. That kind of man. вАЬWell? How are you?вАЭ

вАЬGlad,вАЭ I said, вАЬglad itвАЩs no worse. Glad itвАЩs only you. I was afraid of a purple beard.вАЭ

вАЬAnd how did you get here?вАЭ A man given to muttering, that. One could hear what he said or not just as one pleased. One couldnвАЩt, you understand, be afraid of Conrad Masters.

вАЬMasters, the fight IвАЩve had with this Cerberus to see you!вАЭ

вАЬRulesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ must have rules, you know.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ A decidedly undecided man. Soft-speaking but not plausible, a combination peculiarly English. A man of nerves. Shifty without suavityвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and then, suddenly, apt to bite your head off like a very captain of men: вАЬAnd how did you know Mrs.¬†Storm was ill? Here?вАЭ

вАЬOh,вАЭ I said. вАЬWell.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ And I thought of many things. Of Conrad Masters, of вАЬShould a doctor tell?вАЭ of Cherry-Marvel, that confidant at third-hand, of Mrs.¬†Conrad Masters. A dashing lady, that.

вАЬWho but Cherry-Marvel told me!вАЭ said I.

вАЬGod in Heaven, that man!вАЭ

But Iris swept out of my mind her doctorвАЩs problematical indiscretions to his dashing wife.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬIll,вАЭ he muttered. вАЬDecidedly illвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ mm.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬI heard,вАЭ I said desperately, вАЬthat sheвАЩd had a sort of operationвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬThereвАЩs been no operation!вАЭ snapped that captain of men. вАЬSimply maddens a man, the way these things get about.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬWell, IвАЩm only repeating what I heard, Masters. And you canвАЩt hope for secrecy once our friend gets hold of anythingвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬWho said anything about secrecy?вАЭ A dangerous, feline muttering. вАЬI donвАЩt want secrecy.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Silence. Anxieties walked across it arm-in-arm with that lank manвАЩs doubtful heat.

вАЬI say, Masters, is sheвБ†вАФis she very ill? But, of course, if IвАЩm intruding.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Those worried eyes were fixed on the feet stuck far out from the chair on which he lay as though exhausted. The lay-sister appeared to be pottering about in the next room. вАЬThinking of Donna Guel√£ra, are you? HavenвАЩt much faith in me and Martel-Bonnard, have you?вАЭ Faintly amused those worried eyes looked to be. That was that manвАЩs way. You would think he was being shifty with you when he might be just laughing at you.

Some would speak well, very well, of Dr.¬†Masters; whilst others almost libellously, saying that, working as he did with Eugene Martel-Bonnard, the surgeon, he couldnвАЩt be over-scrupulous in advising profitable but unnecessary operations. Martel-BonnardвАЩs wife wore a famous pearl rope, of which it was said that each pearl had been bought at the price of a womanвАЩs life. But a brilliant surgeonвАЩs life. Martel-Bonnard would say, is full of drawbacks. He charged accordingly. I think that he and Mrs.¬†Masters must have bullied Masters every now and thenвБ†вАФnot that he wouldnвАЩt have looked worried in the Elysian Fields. Between them, those three had once made poor Anna Estella Guel√£ra very sorry she had ever left Chile. She was quite well, Martel-Bonnard said she was very ill, he almost killed her, then he saved her, and how he hurt her! вАЬNaturally,вАЭ smiled Martel-Bonnard. вАЬSuch things hurt. But, my friend, she wasвБ†вАФpouf!вБ†вАФbut for me.вАЭ How one would have liked to operate on that sleek little man, unsuccessfully! He despised you if you differed from him, operated on you if you were fool enough, and robbed you according to a special system he had of discounting the exchange. One hundred thousand francs, poor Anna EstellaвАЩs life had cost her that time. And pain, such as falls only to the lot of women!

вАЬBut. Masters, itвАЩs surely not as bad a case as that!вАЭ

вАЬMmвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ not as bad? WellвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ different shall we say?вАЭ

вАЬBut that was an internal operation! You just saidвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬQuite. ThatвАЩs why itвАЩs different.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

Talking with Conrad Masters was like playing a game in which he who made out the most of the otherвАЩs words scored the most points.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But Iris alone here, in this obscure place as full of crucifixes as a cemetery!

вАЬIвАЩm sorry,вАЭ I said, rising from the stool. вАЬIвАЩm intruding.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬYouвАЩre all right,вАЭ he mumbled. вАЬSo you heard about it from that femme fatale, did you? Damn that man! Bla, bla, bla!вАЭ

Those worried but faintly amused eyes were on me. вАЬBeen hearing quite a lot about you lately. Nurses would have your dossier complete by now if they could understand English. You seem to have put your foot in it somewhere. Rather sorry for you if.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

This banteringвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ medical bantering! Only doctors dare do it. вАЬWell, how are we today?вАЭ But by paying close attention to the game I had scored one point. She was delirious. So far, delirious. ThenвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ вАЬif!вАЭ

вАЬMasters,вАЭ I said, вАЬare you telling me that she is dying?вАЭ

вАЬMmвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ he muttered impatiently, and as he jumped up from his chair the rough brown greatcoat seemed to fill the dingy lodge. It smelt of England, that coat. And, protruding from it, that sharp, naked, weary face with the worried eyes.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬLook here, MastersвБ†вАФвАЭ

вАЬHere you are,вАЭ he muttered. I could not understand why he muttered. вАЬHere you areвАЭ until I found a cigarette in one hand and one of those wretched spirit-lighters in the other. A man without conviction even in his ability to strike a match.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬKnown her for years,вАЭ he muttered towards his feet. вАЬAt Deauville that yearвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ terrible for her. Poor child.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬMasters, you said Donna Guel√£ra might die. You know you did. But she didnвАЩt, did she?вАЭ

He looked at me sharply. вАЬIf only sheвАЩd help herself, lift a finger to help herself! ThatвАЩs what beats a man. DoesnвАЩt lift one finger, she doesnвАЩt.вАЭ

вАЬOh!вАЭ I said, trying to look reasonable. But I couldnвАЩt, for the life of me, accommodate myself to the idea of Iris dying. вАЬI suppose this is the crisis, is it, Masters?вАЭ

The rough greatcoat gave one vindictive flounce, filled the room. вАЬCrisis! The way you people talk of crisis this and crisis that! Hear a word once and stick to it through life! вАШWhen does the crisis pass?вАЩ There is no вАШcrisisвАЩ in most of these infernal things. Malaria, pneumonia, a few othersвБ†вАФyes, crisis, know where you are. But in these things the patient just continues ill, two, three, four weeks, might live, might not. Lysis, not crisis. Crisis!вАЭ

вАЬSorry. Lysis.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬOh, here!вАЭ He suddenly began fumbling in an ancient pocketbook, from which he extracted a small folded piece of paper. вАЬMight interest you,вАЭ he muttered.

Scrawled in pencil across the slip of paper were what looked like two names. That indecipherable scrawl! At last I made out the two names: HilaryвАЩs and mine.

вАЬShe said, should either of these two happen somehow to hear I am ill and call, just be nice to them, please. Her very words.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬOh!вАЭ I said. And I went on staring at the slip of paper. It was a rather grubby slip of paper. And those two scrawled names were like a faint cry of loneliness.

вАЬKnown her for years,вАЭ Masters was muttering. вАЬNice! First tells me not to tell anyone, then to be вАШniceвАЩ to you two.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

I gave him back the slip of paper. I donвАЩt know why, and now I wish I hadnвАЩt. I would like to have it now, beside that fiver. вАЬNice, fivers are.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ Thoughtful Iris! She knew her friends, she did. Lying lonely hereвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and having an afterthought about HilaryвБ†вАФand me! вАЬIf they should somehow happen to hear and call.вАЭ Poor Guy hadnвАЩt a mention. She wasnвАЩt for putting any strain on GuyвАЩs lawfulness. But why lawfulness? I looked at Conrad Masters.

вАЬSeptic poisoning,вАЭ said Masters. вАЬThatвАЩs the trouble.вАЭ

That meant very little to me, for never was a man so ill-informed about such things. вАЬBut,вАЭ I said doubtfully to those gentle-worried eyes, and he murmured:

вАЬSure youвАЩre not thinking of ptomaine poisoning? Not that that isnвАЩt quite enough to be going on with.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬPain,вАЭ I said. вАЬGood Lord, pain.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ All I could think of was pain, pain, pain. One can almost feel the stabs of someoneвАЩs pain. Worst of all, one can mentally hear the faint screams of a voice just recognisable. Conrad Masters, the sight of him, reminded me vividly of Anna EstellaвАЩs pain. Once, from a waiting-room, I had heard her screaming. вАЬPain?вАЭ I said.

вАЬOh, noвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ no.вАЭ He weighed the matter. вАЬNothing to speak of. Just keep still, thatвАЩs the main thing. Very still, for weeks and weeks. Long business, you know. But what worries a man is that she doesnвАЩt try to help herself at all. Letting herself goвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ canвАЩt tell whether consciously or not, but somewhere inside her just not caring. IвАЩve been sharp with her.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Nice business for me, isnвАЩt it? Good Lord, nice! If only sheвАЩd take a pull, pull herself togetherвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ someone just give her mind a jab somehow. No good talking, of course. If she wonвАЩt, she wonвАЩt. Lies there, you know, just not caring.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ He was drawing on a fur-lined glove, and it was to that he spoke; almost, one thought, shyly. A curious, complex gentleman. вАЬSheвАЩs said once or twice sheвАЩd like to see you andвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ well, learn you a thing or two. Some stuff about roses and dandelions. You seem to have made a gaffe somewhere, and itвАЩs quite on her mind to tell you about it. Hope IвАЩm not giving anything awayвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ but might do her good just to see you, feel youвАЩre round about. You canвАЩt tell. WeвАЩll see how she is tomorrow. Extraordinary, IвАЩve found it, the way a woman will wake up for a second from days of delirium for no other purpose than to feel lonely.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Not awake now, though. Ill, this evening. CanвАЩt really, you see, be iller if she tried. It will be good news, really good news, if she is alive in the morning. ThatвАЩs as much as I can say. Sorry.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Well, I must snatch some dinner.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

We were outside. The rain had ceased, it was much warmer. The MastersвАЩs Renault, sleek and shining black but for the scarlet wheels, dwarfed my taxi.

Septic poisoning. I began to remember a little about that. I remembered two words which seemed very like вАЬseptic poisoningвАЭ in reports of trials of wretched women who had вАЬoperated.вАЭ Surely, Masters couldnвАЩtвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ she had, after all, trusted meвБ†вАФвАЬbe nice to himвАЭвБ†вАФand I must at once think the worst thing. Oh, God, how foul a thing a manвАЩs mind is, how foul! But, Iris, dear Iris, why is one able to think of these awful things in connection with you!

вАЬThereвАЩs always hope, you know,вАЭ Masters was muttering. вАЬPity you kept your taxi. I could have dropped you. And Donna Guel√£ra didnвАЩt die, did she?вАЭ

But how Anna Estella had desired to live! вАЬDie, me!вАЭ she had later screamed with laughter.

Iris had trusted me. вАЬBe nice to himвАЭвБ†вАФher very words. And I had thought thatвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬMasters, you wonвАЩt mind my coming round again? Perhaps tonight?вАЭ

вАЬSleep here, if you like,вАЭ he smiled. вАЬIвАЩll be coming myself for a second, about midnight. WifeвАЩs got a party. Like to come? Rather good bridge. Well, please yourself.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

II

I agreed with my sister that it was abominably rude of her younger brother to be nearly an hour late to take her out to dinner, especially as she had been ready for at least twenty minutes. She was furious. I said: вАЬThere is a new dance place open. I heard about it from a friend of mine, Mr.¬†Cherry-Marvel. You will meet him, he is charming. This new place is called La Plume de Ma Tante. It has only been open three nights, so it will be very modish for another two. There is a nightingale there.вАЭ

вАЬOne cannot dance to a nightingale.вАЭ

вАЬBut why are you so exclusive?вАЭ

вАЬIt is cruel and beastly to keep a nightingale caged.вАЭ

вАЬDear, it takes a woman who once had a passion for aigrettes and who loves eating lobsters to be so sensitive. But there is probably baser music to supplement this nightingale. There are, in fact, five lovely niggers. The place is called La Plume de Ma Tante so that English people may know exactly where they stand.вАЭ

вАЬYou are so funny tonight, but would you mind not polishing your shoes on my dress? This is a very terrible taxi, and I think men are monstrous. If you were taking any woman but your sister out to dinner you would have chosen the taxi with discretion.вАЭ

вАЬRudolf and Raymonde are the dancers. I do not want to go to The Pen of My Aunt, but for your sake I would go anywhere. After dinner.вАЭ

She was pleased, loving to dance. We walked up the pavement of the rue Royale to the quiet doors of Larue. She said: вАЬI love Rudolf and Raymonde. I saw them dancing at Monte Carlo, and they say American women give him platinum watches from Cartier and that he was a footman in San Francisco, or was that Rudolf Valentino?вАЭ

I said: вАЬI say, do you know anything about septic poisoning?вАЭ

вАЬReally, how callous you are! Do I know anything about it! But I had it!вАЭ

вАЬNo!вАЭ OneвАЩs sister!

вАЬBut of course I had it! It is amazing when oneвАЩs own brother is quite unaware that one has been through endless pain and torture.вАЭ

вАЬNot pain and torture,вАЭ I said. вАЬA little bird told me.вАЭ

вАЬBut I am not responsible for your feathered friends! I was as good as dead, thatвАЩs all I know.вАЭ

вАЬBut, my dear, that was when you were having a baby! I was in Vienna.вАЭ

вАЬSo you said. But, of course, it came on after I had a baby. One does not get septic poisoning for nothing. I nearly died, I can tell you.вАЭ

вАЬVestiaire, monsieur?вАЭ

вАЬвА¶¬†Oh, I see. A baby. After that.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬI have never been so hungry in my life,вАЭ my sister said, вАЬand you talk to me of septic poisoning. I suppose you think you will destroy my appetite and therefore the bill will be less. I will begin with caviar.вАЭ

вАЬSeptic poisoning,вАЭ I said, вАЬdid not kill you, that is the point. You cannot imagine how glad I am. Let us eat caviar.вАЭ

III

La Plume de Ma Tante. Bright green walls splashed with vermilion. A platform at one end, whereon five blackamoors perspired. At the other, a naked woman. She was without hips, according to the fashion for women. Her arms were twined above her head, and raised on the tip of her fingers was a bowl of green malachite from which pink water splashed into a white alabaster basin at her feet. Many English people were present. They would be going to the Riviera, then they would be coming back from the Riviera. Colonel Duck was there, with the quality. Colonel Duck was, no doubt, just returned from some notably swift exploits on the Cresta Run. But he never was so talkative about his outdoor activities. Cherry-Marvel was there, with a great big woman and a nice-looking boy with the hands of a housemaid who was a famous boxer. There was the usual group of Argentines, very well dressed indeed. They talked about le polo. All over the room elderly women were dancing with young men of both sexes. Mio Mi Marianne was there, sitting alone, but I might not speak with her because I was with my sister. A demimondaine will feel insulted if you speak with her when you are with your sister. Two years before Mio Mi Marianne had one night tied a silk handkerchief round her wrist, and it became the fashion for women to tie silk handkerchiefs round their wrists. Then Mio Mi Marianne tied a silk handkerchief round her throat, and that became the fashion. She thought of these things while smoking opium. She sat alone, staring into a glass of Vichy Water. A young American polo-player called Blister went up to her table, and maybe he asked her to dance, but she just looked at him and he went away again. Her eyes were intent on an opium-dream, and she was very happy in the arms of the infinite. Mio Mi Marianne will be found one day lying on the Aubusson carpet of her drawing-room. There will be a hole in the carpet where her cigarette has died out.

A blackamoor beat a warning roll on his drum, the dancers left the floor, the lights dwindled and awoke again in swaying shadows of blue and carmine. A heavily built young man with the face of a murderer danced a tango with a lovely young girl with short golden curls. Then he threw her on the floor, and picked her up again. Rudolf and Raymonde. He did it beautifully. An American woman called the Duchess of Malvern threw Rudolph a pink carnation. The Baron de Belus said harshly: вАЬThat is a white carnation really, but it is blushing at the fuss that women make of Dagoes.вАЭ In a cage clamped to the bright green wall near us was a dumb nightingale. It kept pecking at the floor of its cage, looking at nothing and nobody. I left my sister in Cherry-MarvelвАЩs care. I said to her that he could dance, and next day she was furious.

IV

The burning eyes of the Renault made the grim Boulevard Pierre Abel almost hospitable. That was a conscientious man, Conrad Masters. How glad I was of him at that moment! What had he said about Iris? something about his having known her for years, something about вАЬthat year at DeauvilleвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ terrible for her.вАЭ That would mean, then, that Masters had been there during the Boy Fenwick tragedy. Iris, poor Iris! Such punishmentsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ for what crimes? What crimes deserved such punishments? Iris, poor Iris! But she wouldnвАЩt mind dying, not she. That was the trouble, Masters had said. But no doubt she knew best.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

The Paradis prison was a pit of blackness in the night. The dim lights behind the iron-barred windows were out, and it was impossible not to wonder if they slept up there in their iron cages, the wicked, the foolish, the betrayed. Perhaps the nightingale in its cage did not care. Perhaps those up there did not care, and slept like angels. But the wrongly accused would not sleep, that was certain. Does innocence wrongly-accused profit anyone except a very wise man or a very good man, except a man who cares nothing for the opinion of this world or one who cares only for the love of the next? I said to the taxi-driver: вАЬHell can know no torment like the agony of an innocent in a cage,вАЭ and when he had carefully examined his tip he agreed with me.

Gently as I could, I rang the bell, praying that the old woman would not be angry with me.

вАЬAha!вАЭ she chuckled. вАЬAha! Monsieur-toujours-de-lвАЩaudace! Mais entrez, monsieur, entrez! The doctor is just this moment arrived. Truly he is a good man, this Dr.¬†MastaireвБ†вАФbut our French doctors, you should see! They come for a moment, they go, and she lives or she dies, what do they care as long as they are paid? But this English doctor, he does not know how to make money easily. Madame his wife was this moment telephoning that he should go home quickly, for they are awaiting him for le bridge. Ah, cet bridge, bridge, bridge!вАЭ

вАЬBut you see how anxious I am! Have you heard anything since I last saw you?вАЭ

вАЬTo have heard nothing, young man, is to have heard good news. But sit down, the doctor himself will tell you in one momentвБ†вАФвАЭ That demoniac bell! It clanged through the place. Perhaps of all the nations in the world the French alone are capable of fixing the loudest possible bell to a nursing-home. The fat old woman grinned vindictively at me. We had been enemies, now we were allies against the intruder. вАЬBah!вАЭ she said, and opened the door.
